front: Poem: ends "you'll be a Man, my son!" and advises to "keep your head when all about you are losing theirs"
Poem: calls Triumph and Disaster "two impostors"
Poem: written for Queen Victoria's Diamond Jubilee
Poem: refrain "Lest we forget -- Lest we forget!"
Poem: "all our pomp of yesterday / is one with Nineveh and Tyre"
Poem: Indian water-bearer saves the narrator's life, dies, and is called "a better man than I am"
Poem: the title character is introduced as a "limpin' lump o' brick-dust" and will be "squattin' on the coals / Givin' drink to poor damned souls"
Poem: speaker imagines a Burmese girl "by the old Moulmein Pagoda" where "dawn comes up like thunder"
Poem: opens "East is East and West is West, and never the twain shall meet"
back: "The Ballad of East and West" (Rudyard Kipling)

front: Poem: Colonel's son chases the horse-thief Kamal
back: "The Ballad of East and West" (Rudyard Kipling)

front: Poem: a soldier set to be executed for murdering a comrade
Poem: refrain about hanging "in the mornin'"
Poem: title drunken soldier complains "it's Tommy this, an' Tommy that, an' 'Tommy, go away'"
Short Story: Dravot and Carnehan try to rule Kafiristan
back: "The Man Who Would Be King" (Rudyard Kipling)

front: Short Story: Dravot is exposed as mortal when his bride bites him and draws blood
back: "The Man Who Would Be King" (Rudyard Kipling)

front: Short Story: narrator told to deliver message "He has gone South for the week" at Marwar Junction
back: "The Man Who Would Be King" (Rudyard Kipling)

front: Short Story: Carnehan survives crucifixion and retrieves Dravot's head after their Kafiristan scheme fails
back: "The Man Who Would Be King" (Rudyard Kipling)

front: Short Story: heroic mongoose defeats cobras Nag and Nagaina
Short Story: Darzee's wife feigns a broken wing to access a melon-patch nest
Novel: son of an Irish soldier lives on the streets of Lahore
Novel: travels with a Tibetan lama searching for a sacred river
Novel: assists the "Great Game"
Novel: originally subtitled "Banker"; spoiled Harvey Cheyne joins Disko Troop's fishing schooner on the Grand Banks
Author: George Orwell called this writer a "good bad poet"
Author: Dan and Una meet the title character after performing A Midsummer Night's Dream; includes "Weland's Sword"
Author: recurring soldiers Learoyd, Otheris, and Mulvaney appear in this collection from this author's "Seven Years' Hard" period
back: Plain Tales from the Hills (Rudyard Kipling)

front: Work: Mowgli the 'man-cub' raised by wolves, mentored by Baloo the bear and Bagheera the panther, uses fire to drive off tiger Shere Khan
Work: Kala Nag takes Little Toomai to see elephants dance in the forest
Work: Kaa the serpent hypnotizes the Bandar-log monkeys who kidnap Mowgli
Work: in The Buddha of Suburbia, Karim is asked to play Mowgli in a stage adaptation of this collection
Novel: opens with the title character sitting on the great cannon Zam-Zamma outside the Lahore Museum
Novel: the protagonist's father prophesied his destiny lies with a red bull on a green field; a trader teaches him the 'Play of the Jewels' memory game
Short Story: they start a Freemason lodge at the Temple of Imbra and defend archers with Martini-Henry rifles
back: "The Man Who Would Be King" (Rudyard Kipling)

front: Short Story: they pass through the Khyber Pass disguised as toy salesmen; their contract forbids liquor and women
back: "The Man Who Would Be King" (Rudyard Kipling)

front: Author: Britain's first Nobel laureate in literature
Author: designed the 'ritual of the calling' for Canadian engineers, ending with the Iron Ring ceremony
